patches.hypothesis: The method was incorrectly handling the year field for dates before the year 0.
patches.changes:
- yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR);
+ yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.AD ? calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR) : 1 - calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR);
patches.diff:
- yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR);
+ yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.AD ? calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR) : 1 - calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR);
patches.original_code:
//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The `fromCalendarFields` method was not properly handling the `ERA` field of the `GregorianCalendar` object.
patches.changes: The `fromCalendarFields` method was updated to properly handle the `ERA` field of the `GregorianCalendar` object.
patches.diff: The `fromCalendarFields` method was updated to properly handle the `ERA` field of the `GregorianCalendar` object.

//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The method was incorrectly assuming that the calendar year was positive, and not accounting for the BC era.
patches.original_code:
//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The method was not handling the case where the input calendar has an era of BC correctly
patches.changes: The method logic was updated to handle the BC era by subtracting the input year from 1.
patches.diff: The method logic was updated to handle the BC era by subtracting the input year from 1.
patches.original_code:
//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The issue is caused by the fact that the `fromCalendarFields` method is ignoring the era of the calendar and always using the current era, which results in a wrong date for years before 1 CE.
patches.changes: The method now takes into account the era of the calendar and adjusts the year accordingly.
patches.diff: The method now takes into account the era of the calendar and adjusts the year accordingly.

patches.hypothesis: The method was not taking into account the era of the calendar, and was always using the absolute year value.
The method was not properly handling the era field of the calendar object.
